one_line_takeaway: A six-component, fully on-device agentic negotiation stack that keeps users' financial constraints local via zk-SNARKs, TEE attestation, and a simulation-critic, reporting 87% success and 94% less cryptographic leakage than cloud baselines in simulation, but with unaddressed behavioral-inference side channels and no real-world validation.
key_finding: The full system reaches an average 87% negotiation success rate with a 2.4x latency reduction and 94% lower cryptographic data exposure (14 bits vs 256 bits) relative to a Cloud-Only baseline, while the distilled 500M on-device model retains ~93.6% of the 7B teacher's accuracy (88% vs 94%) at a 5.5x high-end speedup. Ablations show the negotiation protocol (-15% success if removed) and safety critic (-9%) are the largest contributors to success; removing the world model raises latency 119% and leakage 21%. Explainable decision trails raise user trust scores 27% (3.2->4.1 / 5).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a device-native (fully on-device) Agentic AI system for privacy-preserving bilateral negotiations in insurance and B2B procurement, so that users' financial constraints (budget, reservation prices) never leave their hardware. Its own contribution is an integrated six-component architecture layered on an eight-stage agentic workflow (Goal Initiation -> Guardrails -> Context Expansion -> Intent Understanding -> Adaptive Planning -> Autonomous Execution -> Real-Time Monitoring -> Outcome Evaluation): (1) Selective State Transfer for cross-device session continuity, (2) Explainable Memory using Merkle-tree/blockchain-anchored cryptographic audit trails, (3) World Model Distillation of a 7B cloud teacher into a 500M on-device student, (4) a Multi-Agent Negotiation Protocol combining a secure two-party feasibility pre-check (Paillier homomorphic comparison), Groth16 zk-SNARK constraint-satisfaction proofs, and TEE (ARM TrustZone/Intel SGX) code attestation, (5) Model-Aware Offloading via multi-objective optimization with a hard privacy constraint, and (6) a Simulation-Critic safety mechanism that rejects unfair/policy-violating actions before execution. The cryptographic primitives themselves (Groth16 zk-SNARKs, Paillier, Merkle trees, Nash/Rubinstein bargaining) are cited prior work; the contribution is their composition into an on-device negotiation stack and the empirical evaluation of that stack.
threat_model: Adversaries and risks the design targets: (1) honest-but-curious centralized servers / cloud providers that would otherwise see users' private financial constraints when routed through them; (2) an untrusted counterparty agent that could learn a party's reservation price (mitigated by zk-SNARKs revealing only the binary pmin<=p<=pmax predicate and by the feasibility pre-check that tests range overlap without disclosing bounds); (3) a counterparty running tampered/uncertified agent code (mitigated by mutual TEE attestation of code hashes against a public registry); (4) implementation errors or adversarial exploitation causing acceptance of over-budget or exploitative deals (mitigated by the simulation-critic). The paper is explicit about residual threats it does NOT stop: behavioral-inference attacks where an adversary observing negotiation dynamics (response timing, concession sequences) infers constraint proximity even though ZK hides the values; the safety critic assumes a rational counterparty and may fail against deliberate manipulation (strategic delays, extreme offers); and offline collusion between parties who coordinate externally and use the agents only to fabricate legitimate-looking audit trails (framed as a social, not technical, limitation, with cryptographic trails offered only for post-hoc fraud detection).
limitations: Evidence is self-reported and entirely from controlled simulations on two public datasets; there is no real-world deployment, no independent adversary red-teaming, and no released code/artifacts, so the security claims (attestation, ZK integration, safety-critic efficacy) are asserted rather than externally verified. The authors themselves flag: only bilateral (two-party) negotiation is supported, no multi-party coordination; the safety critic assumes rational counterparties and may be manipulated; ZK proofs do not prevent behavioral/timing-inference leakage, and the reported 'privacy leakage' metric counts only cryptographic exposure, not behavioral side-channels; offline collusion cannot be prevented; and the 500M model's reasoning depth degrades on complex, multi-parameter negotiations (accuracy drops to 82% vs 91% on simple cases). Proposed mitigations (differential privacy for timing, randomized concession schedules) are left to future work.

datasets_used: Medical Cost Personal dataset (Kaggle) - 1,338 records, premiums $1,121-$63,770, features age/BMI/smoking status/region (insurance domain)
Supply Chain Shipment Pricing dataset (Kaggle) - 10,324 B2B transactions, unit prices with product category/shipment mode/freight cost features
User study: N=45 participants (trust/interpretability); N=150 trials per scenario for system metrics
quantitative_results: Overall: 87% average success rate; 2.4x latency reduction vs cloud; 27% higher trust with decision trails

Baseline comparison (medium complexity): Proposed 87% success / 420ms / 14 bits leakage / 5.0J / 0.86 fairness vs Cloud-Only 76% / 1000ms / 256 bits / 2.0J / 0.82; Device-Only 71% / 350ms / 0 bits / 12.0J / 0.78; Naive Edge 79% / 580ms / 180 bits / 7.5J / 0.80
Privacy: 94% reduction in cryptographic data exposure vs Cloud-Only (14 vs 256 bits); +11% success and +0.04 fairness vs Cloud (***p<0.001)

World model distillation: Teacher 7B/94% acc/1000ms/14GB vs Student 500M/88% acc/1.2GB (180ms high-end, 320ms mid, 580ms low); 93.6% accuracy retention, 5.5x high-end speedup; complex scenarios drop to 82% acc, simple retain 91%

Negotiation protocol: 85-90% success across complexity (87% avg), fairness 0.83-0.88, avg 6.2 rounds, terminates within 10 rounds in 96% of cases; ZK proof gen 80ms (high-end), attestation 45ms/session, feasibility pre-check 120ms overhead adding +8% success
Selective State Transfer: 70-85% compression (78% avg), migration 80-180ms (127ms avg), 85-89% task continuity; state 8MB compressed to 2-4MB
Explainable Memory: write 5.2ms, read 2.1ms, proof gen 12.3ms, 340 bytes/decision; trust 3.2->4.1/5 (+27%***), interpretability 2.1->4.3/5
Model-Aware Offloading: 24% latency reduction and 35% monetary cost reduction vs naive offloading
Safety critic: rejects 9% of proposed actions; manual review confirms 87% of rejections were suboptimal

Ablation deltas (from full system): -Negotiation -15% success/-17% latency; -Safety Critic -9% success; -Feasibility -8% success; -World Model -5% success/+119% latency/+21% privacy leakage; -State Transfer -3% success/+108% latency; -Offloading -2% success/+24% latency/+14% leakage; -Memory 0% perf change (trust only)
Insurance E2E (high-end): 86% avg success, 580ms, 5.2J, 14 bits, 0.85 fairness. B2B E2E: 87% avg success, 527ms, 4.7J, 13 bits, 0.85 fairness
baselines_compared: Device-Only (all processing local, no cloud access)
Cited-but-not-run agentic frameworks in qualitative Table I comparison: AutoGPT, LangChain, MetaGPT, CAMEL
